title: Country Ambassadors
subtitle: Country-level outreach, translation, and institutional relationship-building
summary: A volunteer-first country ambassador model for outreach, translation, and institutional relationship-building.
synopsis: A volunteer-first country ambassador model can strengthen outreach, translation, institutional relationships, and future support structures while staying within clear role boundaries.

slides.title: Why This Role Is Needed
slides.summary: Country-level outreach is hard to centralize well, and local context matters.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for why the ambassador role is needed.
bullets: Funding realities and local institutional pathways differ
slides.speech: International outreach is difficult to centralize well. Each country has different university structures, languages and communication norms, funding realities and economic conditions, and SignWriting communities, leaders, and institutional pathways.
slides.notes: Start with the reason the role exists at all.

slides.title: Role Summary
slides.summary: A country ambassador is a trusted local volunteer who helps connect the initiative to institutions and communities.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for ambassador role summary.
bullets: May later grow into stipend-supported service work
slides.speech: A Country Ambassador is a trusted local volunteer who helps connect the Sutton SignWriting initiative with institutions, educators, researchers, and community leaders in a specific country. This begins as a volunteer service role and may later evolve into a paid stipend position for clearly defined services.

slides.title: Core Goals
slides.summary: Ambassadors help build local trust, improve communication, and strengthen support efforts.
bullets: Gather letters of support and collaboration interest
slides.speech: Country ambassadors help identify universities and institutional contacts, make outreach more culturally and linguistically appropriate, explain local conditions and needs, build trust with educational and research partners, and strengthen the case for grants, gifts, and donations through the University of Iowa.

slides.title: Typical Responsibilities
slides.summary: The work is mostly local mapping, communication, introductions, and follow-up.
slides.speech: Responsibilities may include mapping relevant universities, schools, programs, and community organizations; identifying local champions; helping translate or adapt outreach materials; making introductions; sharing local feedback; and documenting visible SignWriting use and impact in the country.
slides.notes: These are concrete activities, not prestige titles.

slides.title: What Ambassadors Are Not
slides.summary: This role should not be confused with formal institutional authority.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for ambassador role limits.
bullets: No payroll or payment intermediary role
slides.speech: Country ambassadors are not expected to make legal or financial commitments on behalf of the project, independently negotiate institutional agreements, serve as payroll or payment intermediaries, or represent the University of Iowa in a formal administrative capacity unless specifically authorized.

slides.title: Volunteer-First Model
slides.summary: Start simply and visibly, then learn from a small pilot before expanding.
bullets: Provide shared messaging and outreach materials
slides.speech: The initial ambassador structure should be simple: start with volunteer appointments, define country scope clearly, agree on reasonable expected activities, provide shared messaging and outreach materials, and recognize contributions visibly and respectfully.
slides.notes: Keep the barrier to entry low.

slides.title: Path to Stipends
slides.summary: As institutional support grows, modest service-based stipends may become appropriate.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for path to stipends.
bullets: Documentation of local use cases and partnerships
slides.speech: As institutional support grows, ambassadors may be eligible for modest stipends tied to clearly defined services such as translation, localization, outreach coordination, event facilitation, and documentation of national use cases and partnerships.
slides.notes: Compensation should be thoughtful and country-sensitive.

slides.title: Selection Criteria
slides.summary: Strong ambassadors combine community trust, communication skill, and reliability.
bullets: Familiarity with universities and institutional networks
slides.speech: Strong ambassadors are likely to have trusted standing in local SignWriting or Deaf education communities, strong communication skills, familiarity with local universities or institutional networks, the ability to work across language and cultural boundaries, and reliability in documenting conversations and follow-up.
slides.notes: Make this read like a real selection guide.

slides.title: Initial Countries
slides.summary: A practical first cohort can start with a few countries where use and interest are already visible.
bullets: Additional countries with visible use and institutional interest
slides.speech: A practical first cohort could include Brazil, Mexico, South Korea, and additional countries where active SignWriting use and institutional interest are already visible.

slides.title: Support Needed From the Central Team
slides.summary: The central project needs to provide materials, tracking, and coordination support.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for central team support.
slides.speech: To make the model work, the central project should provide a short ambassador description, a simple onboarding note, outreach templates that can be localized, a shared contact and follow-up tracker, and a clear explanation of how ambassador work connects to grants, gifts, donations, and partnerships.
slides.notes: The role depends on good support materials.

slides.title: Recommended Next Step
slides.summary: Recruit a small pilot group of ambassadors in three to five countries and test the model before expanding.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for recommended next step.
bullets: Expand after learning from real use
slides.speech: Recruit a small pilot group of ambassadors in three to five countries and test the model before expanding further.
slides.notes: Finish with a manageable next action.
